sections.content:
Now I see:
- Node 3: color=N, boundary=[[28, 28], [28, 31], [30, 31], [30, 28]] - green body at rows 28-30, cols 28-31
- Node 4: color=b, boundary=[[28, 32], [28, 39], [31, 39], [31, 32], ...] - blue center target at rows 28-31, cols 32-39 (18 pixels)
- Node 7: color=W, boundary=[[31, 28], [31, 31], [35, 31], [35, 28]] - white frame at rows 31-35, cols 28-31 (16 pixels, collected target)
- Node 8: color=b, boundary=[[33, 29], [33, 30], [34, 30], [34, 29]] - blue center at rows 33-34, cols 29-30 (inside node 7)
- Node 1: color=c, boundary=[[24, 40], [24, 43], [31, 43], [31, 40]] - charcoal frame at rows 24-31, cols 40-43
- Node 10: color=c, boundary=[[63, 47], [63, 63]] - bottom bar with 17 cells
So the SPACE action collected the target at rows 32-35, cols 28-31. The frame changed from dark gray (G) to white (W). The player didn't move (DOWN had 0 cells changed), but SPACE still collected the target. This suggests the player can collect targets without moving to them, as long as they're adjacent or overlapping.
Wait, the player is at rows 28-30, cols 28-31, and the target was at rows 32-35, cols 28-31. They're not overlapping (rows 28-30 vs 32-35), but they're adjacent (row 30 is adjacent to row 32... no, row 31 is between them). Actually, the player's white strip is at row 31 (node 7 boundary starts at row 31), so the player extends to row 31, which is adjacent to the target at row 32.
Hmm, but the DOWN action had 0 cells changed, meaning the player didn't move. So the player is still at rows 28-30, cols 28-31. The target was at rows 32-35, cols 28-31. They're not adjacent (there's a gap of 1 row: row 31).
Wait, let me reconsider. The player's green body is at rows 28-30, cols 28-31. The white strip (node 7) is at rows 31-35, cols 28-31. But node 7 is the collected target's frame, not the player's white strip. The player's white strip was at rows 28-31, col 27 (or similar) before.
Actually, looking at the previous segmentation, the player had:
- Node 3: color=N, boundary=[[28, 28], [28, 31], [30, 31], [30, 28]] - green body at rows 28-30, cols 28-31
- Node 7: color=W, boundary=[[31, 28], [31, 31]] - white strip at row 31, cols 28-31
So the player's white strip was at row 31, cols 28-31. After collecting the target, node 7 is now the white frame at rows 31-35, cols 28-31 (16 pixels). The player's white strip is no longer visible as a separate node.
This is confusing. Let me re-examine the game mechanics.
Actually, I think the player's white strip is part of the player object, and when the player collects a target, the target's frame changes from charcoal/dark gray to white. The player's white strip might be merged with the collected target's white frame.
Let me focus on the key insight: pressing SPACE on the center target spawns a new target, and collecting targets changes their frame color. The goal might be to collect all targets and shrink the center target completely.
The center target is now 18 pixels (was 28 initially). Each time SPACE is pressed on the center target, it shrinks by 10 pixels and spawns a new target. So after 2 more SPACE presses on the center target, it would shrink to 18 - 10 - 10 = -2, which doesn't make sense. Maybe the center target shrinks by a different amount each time.
Let me try pressing SPACE again on the center target to see if it spawns another target.
